title: Getting started with lineagefreq
text: lineagefreq models pathogen lineage frequency dynamics genomic surveillance count data. Given table lineage-resolved sequence counts time, package estimates relative growth advantages, generates short-term frequency forecasts, provides tools evaluating model accuracy. vignette demonstrates core workflow using simulated SARS-CoV-2 surveillance data.

title: Getting started with lineagefreq
text: entry point lfq_data(), validates standardizes count table. minimum input data frame columns date, lineage name, sequence count. function computes frequencies, flags low-count time points, returns validated lfq_data object.

title: Getting started with lineagefreq
text: fit_model() provides unified interface. default engine multinomial logistic regression (MLR). print output shows lineage’s estimated growth rate relative pivot (reference) lineage, auto-selected prevalent lineage early time series.
code: fit <- fit_model(x, engine = "mlr") fit #> Lineage frequency model (mlr) #> 5 lineages, 40 time points #> Date range: 2022-01-08 to 2022-10-08 #> Pivot: "BA.1" #>  #> Growth rates (per 7-day unit): #>   ↑ BA.2: 0.2308 #>   ↑ BA.4/5: 0.4002 #>   ↑ BQ.1: 0.3516 #>   ↑ Other: 0.1506 #>  #> AIC: 9e+05; BIC: 9e+05

title: Getting started with lineagefreq
text: growth_advantage() converts growth rates interpretable metrics. Four output types available. relative Rt 1 indicates lineage growing faster reference. confidence intervals derived Fisher information matrix.
code: ga <- growth_advantage(fit,                        type = "relative_Rt",                        generation_time = 5) ga #> # A tibble: 5 × 6 #>   lineage estimate lower upper type        pivot #>   <chr>      <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <chr>       <chr> #> 1 BA.1        1     1     1    relative_Rt BA.1  #> 2 BA.2        1.18  1.18  1.18 relative_Rt BA.1  #> 3 BA.4/5      1.33  1.33  1.33 relative_Rt BA.1  #> 4 BQ.1        1.29  1.28  1.29 relative_Rt BA.1  #> 5 Other       1.11  1.11  1.11 relative_Rt BA.1

title: Getting started with lineagefreq
text: autoplot() supports four plot types fitted models.
code: autoplot(fit, type = "frequency") autoplot(fit, type = "advantage", generation_time = 5)

title: Getting started with lineagefreq
text: forecast() projects frequencies forward uncertainty quantified parametric simulation.
code: fc <- forecast(fit, horizon = 28) autoplot(fc) #> Warning in ggplot2::scale_x_date(date_labels = "%Y-%m-%d"): A <numeric> value was passed to a Date scale. #> ℹ The value was converted to a <Date> object.

title: Getting started with lineagefreq
text: summarize_emerging() tests lineage statistically significant frequency increases.
code: summarize_emerging(x) #> # A tibble: 4 × 10 #>   lineage first_seen last_seen  n_timepoints current_freq growth_rate p_value #>   <chr>   <date>     <date>            <int>        <dbl>       <dbl>   <dbl> #> 1 BA.2    2022-01-08 2022-10-08           40       0.156      0.00476       0 #> 2 BA.4/5  2022-01-08 2022-10-08           40       0.797      0.0293        0 #> 3 BQ.1    2022-01-08 2022-10-08           40       0.0176     0.0143        0 #> 4 Other   2022-01-08 2022-10-08           40       0.0293    -0.00489       0 #> # ℹ 3 more variables: p_adjusted <dbl>, significant <lgl>, direction <chr>

title: Getting started with lineagefreq
text: Compare multiple engines backtest() — see vignette("model-comparison"). Run full surveillance workflow — see vignette("surveillance-workflow").

title: Comparing modeling engines
text: lineagefreq provides multiple modeling engines unified fit_model() interface. vignette shows compare using built-backtesting framework. Three engines available v0.1.0: mlr: Multinomial logistic regression (frequentist, fast). piantham: Converts MLR growth rates relative reproduction numbers using generation-time approximation. hier_mlr: Hierarchical MLR partial pooling across locations via empirical Bayes shrinkage.

title: Comparing modeling engines
code: library(lineagefreq)

title: Comparing modeling engines
text: default engine fits multinomial logistic regression one growth rate parameter per non-reference lineage.
code: data(sarscov2_us_2022) x <- lfq_data(sarscov2_us_2022,               lineage = variant, date = date,               count = count, total = total)  fit_mlr <- fit_model(x, engine = "mlr") growth_advantage(fit_mlr, type = "growth_rate") #> # A tibble: 5 × 6 #>   lineage estimate lower upper type        pivot #>   <chr>      <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <chr>       <chr> #> 1 BA.1       0     0     0     growth_rate BA.1  #> 2 BA.2       0.231 0.229 0.232 growth_rate BA.1  #> 3 BA.4/5     0.400 0.398 0.402 growth_rate BA.1  #> 4 BQ.1       0.352 0.346 0.357 growth_rate BA.1  #> 5 Other      0.151 0.149 0.152 growth_rate BA.1

title: Comparing modeling engines
text: Piantham engine wraps MLR translates growth rates relative effective reproduction numbers using specified mean generation time.
code: fit_pian <- fit_model(x, engine = "piantham",                       generation_time = 5) growth_advantage(fit_pian, type = "relative_Rt",                  generation_time = 5) #> # A tibble: 5 × 6 #>   lineage estimate lower upper type        pivot #>   <chr>      <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <chr>       <chr> #> 1 BA.1        1     1     1    relative_Rt BA.1  #> 2 BA.2        1.18  1.18  1.18 relative_Rt BA.1  #> 3 BA.4/5      1.33  1.33  1.33 relative_Rt BA.1  #> 4 BQ.1        1.29  1.28  1.29 relative_Rt BA.1  #> 5 Other       1.11  1.11  1.11 relative_Rt BA.1

title: Comparing modeling engines
text: glance() returns one-row summary model. Since Piantham wrapper around MLR, log-likelihood AIC identical.
code: dplyr::bind_rows(   glance.lfq_fit(fit_mlr),   glance.lfq_fit(fit_pian) ) #> # A tibble: 2 × 10 #>   engine   n_lineages n_timepoints   nobs    df   logLik     AIC     BIC pivot #>   <chr>         <int>        <int>  <int> <int>    <dbl>   <dbl>   <dbl> <chr> #> 1 mlr               5           40 461424     8 -465911. 931838. 931852. BA.1  #> 2 piantham          5           40 461424     8 -465911. 931838. 931852. BA.1  #> # ℹ 1 more variable: convergence <int>

title: Comparing modeling engines
text: backtest() function implements rolling-origin evaluation. origin date, model fit past data forecasts compared held-future observations.

title: Comparing modeling engines
text: score_forecasts() computes standardized accuracy metrics.
code: sc <- score_forecasts(bt,   metrics = c("mae", "coverage")) sc #> # A tibble: 6 × 4 #>   engine   horizon metric     value #>   <chr>      <int> <chr>      <dbl> #> 1 piantham       7 mae      0.00255 #> 2 piantham       7 coverage 0.613   #> 3 piantham      14 mae      0.00272 #> 4 piantham      14 coverage 0.673   #> 5 piantham      21 mae      0.00291 #> 6 piantham      21 coverage 0.703

title: Comparing modeling engines
text: compare_models() summarizes scores per engine, sorted MAE.
code: compare_models(sc, by = c("engine", "horizon")) #> # A tibble: 3 × 4 #>   engine   horizon     mae coverage #>   <chr>      <int>   <dbl>    <dbl> #> 1 piantham       7 0.00255    0.613 #> 2 piantham      14 0.00272    0.673 #> 3 piantham      21 0.00291    0.703

title: Comparing modeling engines
code: plot_backtest(sc)

title: Comparing modeling engines
text: data spans multiple locations unequal sequencing depth, hier_mlr shrinks location-specific estimates toward global mean. stabilizes estimates low-data locations. demonstration requires multi-location data, built-single-location dataset provide. See ?fit_model example simulated multi-location data.

title: Surveillance workflow
text: vignette demonstrates complete end--end surveillance analysis: raw count data actionable outputs. workflow mirrors public health genomics team run weekly.

title: Surveillance workflow
code: library(lineagefreq)  data(sarscov2_us_2022) x <- lfq_data(sarscov2_us_2022,               lineage = variant,               date    = date,               count   = count,               total   = total)

title: Surveillance workflow
text: Real surveillance data often contains dozens low-frequency lineages. collapse_lineages() merges threshold “” category.
code: x_clean <- collapse_lineages(x, min_freq = 0.02) #> Collapsing 1 rare lineage into "Other". attr(x_clean, "lineages") #> [1] "BA.1"   "BA.2"   "BA.4/5" "Other"

title: Surveillance workflow
code: fit <- fit_model(x_clean, engine = "mlr") summary(fit) #> Lineage Frequency Model Summary #> ================================ #> Engine:       mlr  #> Pivot:        BA.1  #> Lineages:     4  #> Time points:  40  #> Total seqs:   461424  #> Parameters:   6  #> Log-lik:      -455671  #> AIC:          911355  #> BIC:          911365  #>  #> Growth rates (per 7 days): #> # A tibble: 4 × 6 #>   lineage estimate lower upper type        pivot #>   <chr>      <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <chr>       <chr> #> 1 BA.1       0     0     0     growth_rate BA.1  #> 2 BA.2       0.228 0.226 0.229 growth_rate BA.1  #> 3 BA.4/5     0.395 0.393 0.397 growth_rate BA.1  #> 4 Other      0.153 0.152 0.154 growth_rate BA.1

title: Surveillance workflow
code: ga <- growth_advantage(fit,                        type = "relative_Rt",                        generation_time = 5) ga #> # A tibble: 4 × 6 #>   lineage estimate lower upper type        pivot #>   <chr>      <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <chr>       <chr> #> 1 BA.1        1     1     1    relative_Rt BA.1  #> 2 BA.2        1.18  1.18  1.18 relative_Rt BA.1  #> 3 BA.4/5      1.33  1.32  1.33 relative_Rt BA.1  #> 4 Other       1.12  1.11  1.12 relative_Rt BA.1 autoplot(fit, type = "advantage", generation_time = 5)

title: Surveillance workflow
code: emerging <- summarize_emerging(x_clean) emerging[emerging$significant, ] #> # A tibble: 3 × 10 #>   lineage first_seen last_seen  n_timepoints current_freq growth_rate p_value #>   <chr>   <date>     <date>            <int>        <dbl>       <dbl>   <dbl> #> 1 BA.2    2022-01-08 2022-10-08           40       0.156      0.00476       0 #> 2 BA.4/5  2022-01-08 2022-10-08           40       0.797      0.0293        0 #> 3 Other   2022-01-08 2022-10-08           40       0.0469    -0.00442       0 #> # ℹ 3 more variables: p_adjusted <dbl>, significant <lgl>, direction <chr>

title: Surveillance workflow
code: fc <- forecast(fit, horizon = 28) autoplot(fc) #> Warning in ggplot2::scale_x_date(date_labels = "%Y-%m-%d"): A <numeric> value was passed to a Date scale. #> ℹ The value was converted to a <Date> object.

title: Surveillance workflow
text: many sequences needed reliably detect lineage 2% frequency?
code: sequencing_power(   target_precision = 0.05,   current_freq = c(0.01, 0.02, 0.05, 0.10) ) #> # A tibble: 4 × 4 #>   current_freq target_precision required_n ci_level #>          <dbl>            <dbl>      <dbl>    <dbl> #> 1         0.01             0.05         16     0.95 #> 2         0.02             0.05         31     0.95 #> 3         0.05             0.05         73     0.95 #> 4         0.1              0.05        139     0.95

title: Surveillance workflow
text: results compatible broom ecosystem.
code: tidy.lfq_fit(fit) #> # A tibble: 6 × 6 #>   lineage term        estimate std.error conf.low conf.high #>   <chr>   <chr>          <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl> #> 1 BA.2    intercept     -2.97   0.0109     -2.99     -2.95  #> 2 BA.2    growth_rate    0.228  0.000750    0.226     0.229 #> 3 BA.4/5  intercept     -7.88   0.0238     -7.93     -7.83  #> 4 BA.4/5  growth_rate    0.395  0.00103     0.393     0.397 #> 5 Other   intercept     -1.53   0.00823    -1.55     -1.52  #> 6 Other   growth_rate    0.153  0.000677    0.152     0.154 glance.lfq_fit(fit) #> # A tibble: 1 × 10 #>   engine n_lineages n_timepoints   nobs    df   logLik     AIC     BIC pivot #>   <chr>       <int>        <int>  <int> <int>    <dbl>   <dbl>   <dbl> <chr> #> 1 mlr             4           40 461424     6 -455671. 911355. 911365. BA.1  #> # ℹ 1 more variable: convergence <int>

title: Surveillance workflow
text: typical weekly workflow: lfq_data() — ingest new counts collapse_lineages() — clean rare lineages fit_model() — estimate dynamics summarize_emerging() — flag growing lineages forecast() — project forward 4 weeks autoplot() — generate report figures

what: Lineage Frequency Dynamics and Growth-Advantage Estimation from Genomic Surveillance Counts
title: Lineage Frequency Dynamics and Growth-Advantage Estimation from Genomic Surveillance Counts
text: Lineage Frequency Dynamics Growth-Advantage Estimation Genomic Surveillance Counts lineagefreq models pathogen lineage frequency dynamics genomic surveillance count data. provides unified interface multiple modeling engines, rolling-origin backtesting, standardized forecast scoring, emergence detection, sequencing power analysis.

title: Lineage Frequency Dynamics and Growth-Advantage Estimation from Genomic Surveillance Counts
text: Install development version GitHub:
code: # install.packages("devtools") devtools::install_github("CuiweiG/lineagefreq")

title: Lineage Frequency Dynamics and Growth-Advantage Estimation from Genomic Surveillance Counts
code: library(lineagefreq)  # Load built-in simulated SARS-CoV-2 data data(sarscov2_us_2022)  # Prepare data x <- lfq_data(sarscov2_us_2022,               lineage = variant, date = date,               count = count, total = total)  # Fit multinomial logistic regression fit <- fit_model(x, engine = "mlr")  # Extract growth advantages growth_advantage(fit, type = "relative_Rt", generation_time = 5)  # Forecast 4 weeks ahead fc <- forecast(fit, horizon = 28) autoplot(fc)  # Detect emerging lineages summarize_emerging(x)

title: Lineage Frequency Dynamics and Growth-Advantage Estimation from Genomic Surveillance Counts
text: Unified modeling: fit_model() engines "mlr", "hier_mlr", "piantham". Growth advantage: four output types (growth rate, relative Rt, selection coefficient, doubling time). Forecasting: parametric simulation prediction intervals. Backtesting: backtest() rolling origins across multiple engines horizons. Scoring: MAE, RMSE, coverage, weighted interval score via score_forecasts(). Surveillance tools: summarize_emerging(), sequencing_power(), collapse_lineages(). broom compatible: tidy(), glance(), augment(). Publication-ready plots: autoplot() fits, forecasts, backtest results.

title: Lineage Frequency Dynamics and Growth-Advantage Estimation from Genomic Surveillance Counts
text: lineagefreq works pathogen variant/lineage-resolved sequencing count data, including SARS-CoV-2, influenza, RSV, others.

what: Rolling-origin backtesting of lineage frequency models — backtest
title: Rolling-origin backtesting of lineage frequency models — backtest
text: Evaluates forecast accuracy repeatedly fitting models historical data comparing predictions held-observations. implements evaluation framework described Abousamra et al. (2024).

title: Rolling-origin backtesting of lineage frequency models — backtest
code: backtest(   data,   engines = "mlr",   origins = "weekly",   horizons = c(7L, 14L, 21L, 28L),   min_train = 42L,   ... )

title: Rolling-origin backtesting of lineage frequency models — backtest
text: data lfq_data object. engines Character vector engine names compare. Default "mlr". origins select forecast origins: "weekly" (default): one origin per unique date, starting min_train days. integer: use every Nth date origin. Date vector: use specific dates origins. horizons Integer vector forecast horizons days. Default c(7, 14, 21, 28). min_train Minimum training window days. Origins earlier min(date) + min_train skipped. Default 42 (6 weeks). ... Additional arguments passed fit_model() (e.g., generation_time Piantham engine).

title: Rolling-origin backtesting of lineage frequency models — backtest
text: lfq_backtest object (tibble subclass) columns: origin_date Date used training cutoff. target_date Date predicted. horizon Forecast horizon days. engine Engine name. lineage Lineage name. predicted Predicted frequency (median). lower Lower prediction bound. upper Upper prediction bound. observed Observed frequency target_date.

title: Rolling-origin backtesting of lineage frequency models — backtest

what: Collapse rare lineages into an aggregate group — collapse_lineages
title: Collapse rare lineages into an aggregate group — collapse_lineages
text: Merges lineages never exceed frequency count threshold single group (default ""). Useful reducing noise dozens low-frequency lineages.

title: Collapse rare lineages into an aggregate group — collapse_lineages
code: collapse_lineages(   x,   min_freq = 0.01,   min_count = 10L,   other_label = "Other",   mapping = NULL )

title: Collapse rare lineages into an aggregate group — collapse_lineages
text: x lfq_data object. min_freq Minimum peak frequency lineage must reach time point kept. Default 0.01 (1%). min_count Minimum total count across time points kept. Default 10. other_label Label collapsed group. Default "". mapping Optional named character vector custom grouping. Names = original lineage names, values = group names. provided, min_freq min_count ignored.

title: Collapse rare lineages into an aggregate group — collapse_lineages
text: lfq_data object rare lineages merged.

title: Collapse rare lineages into an aggregate group — collapse_lineages
code: sim <- simulate_dynamics(n_lineages = 6,   advantages = c(A = 1.3, B = 1.1, C = 0.95, D = 0.5, E = 0.3),   n_timepoints = 12, seed = 1) collapsed <- collapse_lineages(sim, min_freq = 0.05) attr(collapsed, "lineages") #> [1] "A"   "B"   "C"   "D"   "E"   "ref"

what: Compare model engines from backtest scores — compare_models
title: Compare model engines from backtest scores — compare_models
text: Summarises ranks engines across horizons based forecast accuracy scores.

title: Compare model engines from backtest scores — compare_models
code: compare_models(scores, by = "engine")

title: Compare model engines from backtest scores — compare_models
text: scores Output score_forecasts(). Grouping variable(s). Default "engine".

title: Compare model engines from backtest scores — compare_models
text: tibble average scores per group, sorted MAE.

title: Compare model engines from backtest scores — compare_models
code: # \donttest{ sim <- simulate_dynamics(n_lineages = 3,   advantages = c("A" = 1.2, "B" = 0.8),   n_timepoints = 20, seed = 1) bt <- backtest(sim, engines = "mlr",   horizons = c(7, 14), min_train = 42) sc <- score_forecasts(bt) compare_models(sc) #> # A tibble: 1 × 5 #>   engine     mae    rmse coverage     wis #>   <chr>    <dbl>   <dbl>    <dbl>   <dbl> #> 1 mlr    0.00709 0.00936    0.510 0.00169 # }

title: Filter sparse time points and lineages — filter_sparse
text: x lfq_data object. min_total Minimum total sequences per time point. Default 10. min_timepoints Minimum number time points lineage must appear retained. Default 3.

text: lfq_data object sparse entries removed.

title: Filter sparse time points and lineages — filter_sparse
code: sim <- simulate_dynamics(n_lineages = 3,   advantages = c("A" = 1.2, "B" = 0.8), seed = 1) filtered <- filter_sparse(sim, min_total = 100)

what: Fit a lineage frequency model — fit_model
title: Fit a lineage frequency model — fit_model
text: Unified interface modeling lineage frequency dynamics. Supports multiple engines share input/output contract.

title: Fit a lineage frequency model — fit_model
code: fit_model(   data,   engine = c("mlr", "hier_mlr", "piantham"),   pivot = NULL,   horizon = 28L,   ci_level = 0.95,   ... )

title: Fit a lineage frequency model — fit_model
text: data lfq_data object. engine Model engine use: "mlr" (default): Multinomial logistic regression. Fast, frequentist, external dependencies. "hier_mlr": Hierarchical MLR partial pooling across locations. Requires .location column data. "piantham": Piantham approximation converting MLR growth rates relative reproduction numbers. Requires generation_time argument. pivot Reference lineage name. Growth rates reported relative lineage (fixed 0). Default: lineage highest count earliest time point. horizon Forecast horizon days (stored later use forecast()). Default 28. ci_level Confidence level intervals. Default 0.95. ... Engine-specific arguments passed internal engine function. engine = "mlr": window, ci_method, laplace_smooth. engine = "piantham": generation_time (required). engine = "hier_mlr": shrinkage_method.

title: Fit a lineage frequency model — fit_model
text: lfq_fit object (S3 class), list containing: engine Engine name (character). growth_rates Named numeric vector growth rates per time_scale days (pivot = 0). intercepts Named numeric vector intercepts. pivot Name pivot lineage. lineages Character vector lineage names. fitted_values Tibble fitted frequencies. residuals Tibble observed, fitted, Pearson residuals. vcov_matrix Variance-covariance matrix. loglik, aic, bic Model fit statistics. nobs, n_timepoints, df Sample model sizes. ci_level, horizon specified. call matched call.

title: Fit a lineage frequency model — fit_model
text: Abousamra E, Figgins M, Bedford T (2024). Fitness models provide accurate short-term forecasts SARS-CoV-2 variant frequency. PLoS Computational Biology, 20(9):e1012443. doi:10.1371/journal.pcbi.1012443

title: Fit a lineage frequency model — fit_model
code: sim <- simulate_dynamics(   n_lineages = 3,   advantages = c("JN.1" = 1.3, "KP.3" = 0.9),   n_timepoints = 15, seed = 42 ) fit <- fit_model(sim, engine = "mlr") fit #> Lineage frequency model (mlr) #> 3 lineages, 15 time points #> Date range: 2026-03-27 to 2026-07-03 #> Pivot: "KP.3" #>  #> Growth rates (per 7-day unit): #>   ↑ JN.1: 0.3638 #>   ↑ ref: 0.1122 #>  #> AIC: 9450; BIC: 9453

text: Forecast lineage frequencies (generic)

title: Forecast lineage frequencies (generic) — forecast
code: forecast(object, ...)

title: Forecast lineage frequencies (generic) — forecast
text: object model object. ... Additional arguments passed methods.

title: Forecast lineage frequencies (generic) — forecast
text: forecast object.

title: Forecast lineage frequencies — forecast.lfq_fit
text: Projects lineage frequencies forward time using fitted model. Prediction uncertainty quantified parametric simulation estimated parameter distribution.

title: Forecast lineage frequencies — forecast.lfq_fit
code: # S3 method for class 'lfq_fit' forecast(object, horizon = 28L, ci_level = 0.95, n_sim = 1000L, ...)

title: Forecast lineage frequencies — forecast.lfq_fit
text: object lfq_fit object. horizon Number days forecast. Default 28 (4 weeks). ci_level Confidence level prediction intervals. Default 0.95. n_sim Number parameter draws prediction intervals. Default 1000. ... Unused.

title: Forecast lineage frequencies — forecast.lfq_fit
text: lfq_forecast object (tibble subclass) columns: .date Date. .lineage Lineage name. .median Median predicted frequency. .lower Lower prediction bound. .upper Upper prediction bound. .type "fitted" "forecast".

title: Forecast lineage frequencies — forecast.lfq_fit
code: # \donttest{ sim <- simulate_dynamics(n_lineages = 3,   advantages = c("A" = 1.2, "B" = 0.8), seed = 1) fit <- fit_model(sim, engine = "mlr") fc <- forecast(fit, horizon = 21) fc #>  #> ── Lineage frequency forecast  #> Engine: mlr #> Forecast start: 2026-08-08 | Horizon: 21 days #> CI level: 95% #> 60 fitted + 9 forecast rows #>  #> # A tibble: 69 × 6 #>    .date      .lineage .median .lower .upper .type  #>  * <date>     <chr>      <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl> <chr>  #>  1 2026-03-27 A          0.341  0.341  0.341 fitted #>  2 2026-03-27 B          0.324  0.324  0.324 fitted #>  3 2026-03-27 ref        0.335  0.335  0.335 fitted #>  4 2026-04-03 A          0.408  0.408  0.408 fitted #>  5 2026-04-03 B          0.257  0.257  0.257 fitted #>  6 2026-04-03 ref        0.335  0.335  0.335 fitted #>  7 2026-04-10 A          0.476  0.476  0.476 fitted #>  8 2026-04-10 B          0.198  0.198  0.198 fitted #>  9 2026-04-10 ref        0.326  0.326  0.326 fitted #> 10 2026-04-17 A          0.541  0.541  0.541 fitted #> # ℹ 59 more rows # }

title: Extract growth advantage estimates — growth_advantage
text: Computes relative fitness lineage fitted model. Supports four output formats different use cases.

title: Extract growth advantage estimates — growth_advantage
code: growth_advantage(   fit,   type = c("growth_rate", "relative_Rt", "selection_coefficient", "doubling_time"),   generation_time = NULL,   ci_level = NULL )

title: Extract growth advantage estimates — growth_advantage
text: fit lfq_fit object returned fit_model(). type Output type: "growth_rate" (default): raw growth rate delta per time_scale days (typically per week). "relative_Rt": relative effective reproduction number. Requires generation_time. "selection_coefficient": relative Rt minus 1. Requires generation_time. "doubling_time": days frequency ratio vs pivot double (positive = growing) halve (negative = declining). generation_time Mean generation time days. Required type = "relative_Rt" "selection_coefficient". Common values: SARS-CoV-2 approximately 5 days, influenza approximately 3 days. ci_level Confidence level intervals. Default uses level fitted model.

title: Extract growth advantage estimates — growth_advantage
text: tibble columns: lineage Lineage name. estimate Point estimate. lower Lower confidence bound. upper Upper confidence bound. type Type estimate. pivot Name pivot (reference) lineage.

title: Extract growth advantage estimates — growth_advantage
code: sim <- simulate_dynamics(   n_lineages = 3,   advantages = c("JN.1" = 1.3, "KP.3" = 0.9),   n_timepoints = 15, seed = 42 ) fit <- fit_model(sim, engine = "mlr")  # Growth rates per week growth_advantage(fit, type = "growth_rate") #> # A tibble: 3 × 6 #>   lineage estimate  lower upper type        pivot #>   <chr>      <dbl>  <dbl> <dbl> <chr>       <chr> #> 1 JN.1       0.364 0.338  0.390 growth_rate KP.3  #> 2 KP.3       0     0      0     growth_rate KP.3  #> 3 ref        0.112 0.0834 0.141 growth_rate KP.3   # Relative Rt (needs generation time) growth_advantage(fit, type = "relative_Rt", generation_time = 5) #> # A tibble: 3 × 6 #>   lineage estimate lower upper type        pivot #>   <chr>      <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <chr>       <chr> #> 1 JN.1        1.30  1.27  1.32 relative_Rt KP.3  #> 2 KP.3        1     1     1    relative_Rt KP.3  #> 3 ref         1.08  1.06  1.11 relative_Rt KP.3

what: Create a lineage frequency data object — lfq_data
title: Create a lineage frequency data object — lfq_data
text: Validates, structures, annotates lineage count data downstream modeling analysis. entry point lineagefreq workflows.

title: Create a lineage frequency data object — lfq_data
code: lfq_data(   data,   lineage,   date,   count,   total = NULL,   location = NULL,   min_total = 10L )

title: Create a lineage frequency data object — lfq_data
text: data data frame containing minimum columns lineage identity, date, count. lineage <tidy-select> Column containing lineage/variant identifiers (character factor). date <tidy-select> Column containing collection dates (Date class parseable character). count <tidy-select> Column containing sequence counts (non-negative integers). total <tidy-select> Optional column total sequences per date-location. NULL, computed sum count per group. location <tidy-select> Optional column geographic stratification. min_total Minimum total count per time point. Time points flagged unreliable. Default 10.

title: Create a lineage frequency data object — lfq_data
text: lfq_data object (tibble subclass) standardized columns: .lineage Lineage identifier (character). .date Collection date (Date). .count Sequence count (integer). .total Total sequences time point (integer). .freq Observed frequency (numeric). .reliable Logical; TRUE .total >= min_total. .location Location, provided (character). original columns preserved.

title: Create a lineage frequency data object — lfq_data
text: Performs following validation processing: Checks required columns exist correct types. Coerces character dates Date via ISO 8601 parsing. Ensures counts non-negative integers. Replaces NA counts 0 (warning). Aggregates duplicate lineage-date rows summing (warning). Computes per-time-point totals frequencies. Flags time points min_total unreliable. Sorts date ascending, lineage alphabetically.

title: Create a lineage frequency data object — lfq_data
code: d <- data.frame(   date = rep(seq(as.Date("2024-01-01"), by = "week",                  length.out = 8), each = 3),   lineage = rep(c("JN.1", "KP.3", "Other"), 8),   n = c(5, 2, 93, 12, 5, 83, 28, 11, 61, 50, 20, 30,         68, 18, 14, 80, 12, 8, 88, 8, 4, 92, 5, 3) ) x <- lfq_data(d, lineage = lineage, date = date, count = n) x #>  #> ── Lineage frequency data  #> 3 lineages, 8 time points #> Date range: 2024-01-01 to 2024-02-19 #> Lineages: "JN.1, KP.3, Other" #>  #> # A tibble: 24 × 6 #>    .date      .lineage .count .total .freq .reliable #>  * <date>     <chr>     <int>  <int> <dbl> <lgl>     #>  1 2024-01-01 JN.1          5    100  0.05 TRUE      #>  2 2024-01-01 KP.3          2    100  0.02 TRUE      #>  3 2024-01-01 Other        93    100  0.93 TRUE      #>  4 2024-01-08 JN.1         12    100  0.12 TRUE      #>  5 2024-01-08 KP.3          5    100  0.05 TRUE      #>  6 2024-01-08 Other        83    100  0.83 TRUE      #>  7 2024-01-15 JN.1         28    100  0.28 TRUE      #>  8 2024-01-15 KP.3         11    100  0.11 TRUE      #>  9 2024-01-15 Other        61    100  0.61 TRUE      #> 10 2024-01-22 JN.1         50    100  0.5  TRUE      #> # ℹ 14 more rows

what: lineagefreq: Lineage Frequency Dynamics from Genomic Surveillance Counts — lineagefreq-package
title: lineagefreq: Lineage Frequency Dynamics from Genomic Surveillance Counts — lineagefreq-package
text: Models pathogen lineage frequency dynamics genomic surveillance count data. Provides unified fit_model() interface multiple engines (MLR, hierarchical MLR, Piantham), rolling-origin backtesting via backtest(), standardized scoring via score_forecasts(), emergence detection, sequencing power analysis.

title: lineagefreq: Lineage Frequency Dynamics from Genomic Surveillance Counts — lineagefreq-package
code: x <- lfq_data(my_counts, lineage = lineage, date = date, count = n) fit <- fit_model(x, engine = "mlr") growth_advantage(fit, generation_time = 5)

Score backtest forecast accuracy — score_forecasts
text: Computes standardized accuracy metrics backtesting results.

title: Score backtest forecast accuracy — score_forecasts
code: score_forecasts(bt, metrics = c("mae", "rmse", "coverage", "wis"))

title: Score backtest forecast accuracy — score_forecasts
text: bt lfq_backtest object backtest(). metrics Character vector metrics compute: "mae": Mean absolute error frequency. "rmse": Root mean squared error. "coverage": Proportion within prediction intervals. "wis": Weighted interval score.

title: Score backtest forecast accuracy — score_forecasts
text: tibble columns: engine, horizon, metric, value.

title: Score backtest forecast accuracy — score_forecasts
code: # \donttest{ sim <- simulate_dynamics(n_lineages = 3,   advantages = c("A" = 1.2, "B" = 0.8),   n_timepoints = 20, seed = 1) bt <- backtest(sim, engines = "mlr",   horizons = c(7, 14), min_train = 42) score_forecasts(bt) #> # A tibble: 8 × 4 #>   engine horizon metric     value #>   <chr>    <int> <chr>      <dbl> #> 1 mlr          7 mae      0.00723 #> 2 mlr          7 rmse     0.00964 #> 3 mlr          7 coverage 0.436   #> 4 mlr          7 wis      0.00189 #> 5 mlr         14 mae      0.00695 #> 6 mlr         14 rmse     0.00909 #> 7 mlr         14 coverage 0.583   #> 8 mlr         14 wis      0.00150 # }

title: Sequencing power analysis — sequencing_power
text: Estimates minimum number sequences needed detect lineage given frequency specified precision.

title: Sequencing power analysis — sequencing_power
code: sequencing_power(target_precision = 0.05, current_freq = 0.02, ci_level = 0.95)

title: Sequencing power analysis — sequencing_power
text: target_precision Desired half-width frequency confidence interval. Default 0.05 (plus/minus 5 percentage points). current_freq True assumed frequency target lineage. Can vector multiple scenarios. Default 0.02 (2%). ci_level Confidence level. Default 0.95.

title: Sequencing power analysis — sequencing_power
text: tibble columns: current_freq, target_precision, required_n, ci_level.

title: Sequencing power analysis — sequencing_power
text: Uses normal approximation binomial: $$n = z^2 \cdot p(1-p) / E^2$$ z critical value, p frequency, E precision.

title: Sequencing power analysis — sequencing_power
code: # How many sequences to estimate a 2% lineage within +/-5%? sequencing_power() #> # A tibble: 1 × 4 #>   current_freq target_precision required_n ci_level #>          <dbl>            <dbl>      <dbl>    <dbl> #> 1         0.02             0.05         31     0.95  # Multiple scenarios sequencing_power(current_freq = c(0.01, 0.02, 0.05, 0.10)) #> # A tibble: 4 × 4 #>   current_freq target_precision required_n ci_level #>          <dbl>            <dbl>      <dbl>    <dbl> #> 1         0.01             0.05         16     0.95 #> 2         0.02             0.05         31     0.95 #> 3         0.05             0.05         73     0.95 #> 4         0.1              0.05        139     0.95

title: Simulate lineage frequency dynamics — simulate_dynamics
text: Generates synthetic lineage frequency data multinomial sampling model configurable growth advantages. Useful model validation, power analysis, teaching.

title: Simulate lineage frequency dynamics — simulate_dynamics
code: simulate_dynamics(   n_lineages = 4L,   n_timepoints = 20L,   total_per_tp = 500L,   advantages = NULL,   reference_name = "ref",   start_date = Sys.Date(),   interval = 7L,   overdispersion = NULL,   seed = NULL )

title: Simulate lineage frequency dynamics — simulate_dynamics
text: n_lineages Number lineages including reference. Default 4. n_timepoints Number time points. Default 20. total_per_tp Sequences per time point. single integer (constant across time) vector length n_timepoints (variable sampling effort). Default 500. advantages Named numeric vector per-week multiplicative growth advantages non-reference lineages. Length must equal n_lineages - 1. Values > 1 mean growing faster reference, < 1 means declining. NULL, random values (0.8, 1.5). reference_name Name reference lineage. Default "ref". start_date Start date time series. Default today. interval Days consecutive time points. Default 7 (weekly data). overdispersion NULL (default), standard multinomial sampling. positive number, Dirichlet-Multinomial sampling concentration = 1 / overdispersion. Larger values = overdispersion. seed Random seed reproducibility.

title: Simulate lineage frequency dynamics — simulate_dynamics
text: lfq_data object additional true_freq column containing true (pre-sampling) frequencies.

title: Simulate lineage frequency dynamics — simulate_dynamics
code: # JN.1 grows 1.3x per week, KP.3 declines at 0.9x sim <- simulate_dynamics(   n_lineages = 3,   advantages = c("JN.1" = 1.3, "KP.3" = 0.9),   n_timepoints = 15,   seed = 42 ) sim #>  #> ── Lineage frequency data  #> 3 lineages, 15 time points #> Date range: 2026-03-27 to 2026-07-03 #> Lineages: "JN.1, KP.3, ref" #>  #> # A tibble: 45 × 7 #>    .date      .lineage .count true_freq .total .freq .reliable #>  * <date>     <chr>     <int>     <dbl>  <int> <dbl> <lgl>     #>  1 2026-03-27 JN.1        156     0.333    500 0.312 TRUE      #>  2 2026-03-27 KP.3        184     0.333    500 0.368 TRUE      #>  3 2026-03-27 ref         160     0.333    500 0.32  TRUE      #>  4 2026-04-03 JN.1        204     0.406    500 0.408 TRUE      #>  5 2026-04-03 KP.3        143     0.281    500 0.286 TRUE      #>  6 2026-04-03 ref         153     0.312    500 0.306 TRUE      #>  7 2026-04-10 JN.1        240     0.483    500 0.48  TRUE      #>  8 2026-04-10 KP.3        120     0.231    500 0.24  TRUE      #>  9 2026-04-10 ref         140     0.286    500 0.28  TRUE      #> 10 2026-04-17 JN.1        307     0.560    500 0.614 TRUE      #> # ℹ 35 more rows

title: Summarize emerging lineages — summarize_emerging
text: Tests whether lineage's frequency significantly increasing time using binomial GLM. Useful early warning lineages may warrant enhanced surveillance.

title: Summarize emerging lineages — summarize_emerging
code: summarize_emerging(data, threshold = 0.01, min_obs = 3L, p_adjust = "holm")

title: Summarize emerging lineages — summarize_emerging
text: data lfq_data object. threshold Minimum current frequency test. Default 0.01. min_obs Minimum time points observed. Default 3. p_adjust P-value adjustment method. Default "holm".

title: Summarize emerging lineages — summarize_emerging
text: tibble columns: lineage, first_seen, last_seen, n_timepoints, current_freq, growth_rate, p_value, p_adjusted, significant, direction.

title: Summarize emerging lineages — summarize_emerging
code: sim <- simulate_dynamics(   n_lineages = 4,   advantages = c(emerging = 1.5, stable = 1.0, declining = 0.7),   n_timepoints = 12, seed = 42) summarize_emerging(sim) #> # A tibble: 2 × 10 #>   lineage  first_seen last_seen  n_timepoints current_freq growth_rate   p_value #>   <chr>    <date>     <date>            <int>        <dbl>       <dbl>     <dbl> #> 1 emerging 2026-03-27 2026-06-12           12        0.976      0.0645 0         #> 2 stable   2026-03-27 2026-06-12           12        0.016     -0.0394 9.90e-101 #> # ℹ 3 more variables: p_adjusted <dbl>, significant <lgl>, direction <chr>

title: lineagefreq 0.1.0
text: Initial CRAN release.
